sections.heading: molecular_classification
sections.content: Vulvar SCC is classified into two major molecular-pathologic pathways: HPV-associated and HPV-independent. This classification has important prognostic and therapeutic implications [MS-2, VULVA-A 1/4].

sections.heading: hpv_associated_pathway
sections.content: HPV-associated vulvar SCC is driven by persistent infection with carcinogenic HPV strains, most commonly HPV-16 (78.1% of HPV-positive disease) and HPV-33 (7.5%) [MS-2]. Estimates of the percentage of vulvar cancers attributable to HPV infection range from conservative estimates of 30% to up to 69%, with a meta-analysis reporting an HPV prevalence of 39.7% [MS-2]. A recent meta-analysis showed the prevalence of HPV in vulvar cancer and VIN to be 39.1% and 76.1%, respectively [MS-2]. HPV-driven transformation results in overexpression of the p16INK4a tumor suppressor protein, which serves as a surrogate biomarker for HPV E7 oncoprotein activity. Immunohistochemistry shows strong, diffuse, block-like positive nuclear and cytoplasmic staining with p16 and wild-type p53 with a heterogeneous staining pattern and basal sparing pattern [VULVA-A 1/4]. HPV-associated SCC has a better prognosis than HPV-independent SCC [MS-2].

sections.heading: hpv_independent_pathway
sections.content: HPV-independent vulvar SCC arises through two distinct molecular subgroups defined by TP53 status: TP53-mutant and TP53 wild-type. TP53-mutant HPV-independent SCC is the most clinically aggressive molecular category. Immunohistochemistry usually shows aberrant p53 staining (widespread, strong nuclear expression or complete absence/null expression) and patchy (negative) p16 staining [VULVA-A 1/4]. The p53-abnormal SCCs have the worst clinical outcomes of the three molecular categories (HPV positive, HPV-negative/p53 mutant, and HPV-negative p53 wild-type) [MS-2]. HPV-independent dVIN (differentiated VIN) is associated with chronic inflammatory conditions such as lichen sclerosus and is characterized by a faster rate of progression to invasive SCC [MS-2].

sections.heading: tumor_suppressor_and_oncogene_dysregulation
sections.content: The TP53 tumor suppressor gene is the primary driver in HPV-independent vulvar SCC. Wild-type p53 shows a heterogeneous staining pattern with basal sparing, while TP53-mutant tumors show either widespread strong nuclear expression (gain-of-function) or complete absence/null expression (loss-of-function) [VULVA-A 1/4]. The p53 IHC has unique features compared to p53 staining patterns encountered in ovarian and endometrial cancers [MS-5]. NCCN guidelines recommend p53 IHC (or NGS as acceptable alternative) to determine TP53 status in HPV-negative tumors [VULVA-A 2/4].

sections.heading: vulvar_melanoma_molecular_alterations
sections.content: Vulvar and vaginal melanomas form a distinct molecular subtype of melanoma. One study evaluating 65 primary vulvar and vaginal melanoma tumors showed that mutations in BRAF are absent in vulvar melanomas, while NRAS mutations and KIT amplifications occur in both vulvar and vaginal melanomas [MS-19]. Another study comparing 51 vulvar and vaginal melanomas with 2253 non-gynecologic melanomas identified 50% BRAF mutations and 22% KIT mutations in vulvovaginal melanoma tumors, compared with 82.1% BRAF mutations and 3% KIT mutations in non-gynecologic melanomas [MS-19]. NRAS mutations were rare compared with other cutaneous melanomas. These data highlight that vulvar and vaginal melanomas involve different molecular alterations compared to cutaneous melanoma [MS-19].

sections.heading: biomarker_testing
sections.content: For advanced, recurrent, or metastatic disease, NCCN recommends: HPV status determination by p16 IHC, RNA sequencing, or HPV ISH; p53 IHC to determine TP53 status in HPV-negative tumors; PD-L1 testing; HER2 IHC testing (with reflex HER2 FISH for equivocal IHC); and comprehensive molecular profiling including MMR/MSI, TMB, and NTRK testing [VULVA-A 2/4].

sections.heading: ntrk_gene_fusions
sections.content: NTRK gene fusions lead to constitutively active tropomyosin receptor kinases (TRKs), which promote cancer development and progression. Approximately 0.3% of solid tumors express NTRK gene fusions [MS-18]. These represent a rare but actionable biomarker for larotrectinib, entrectinib, and repotrectinib [VULVA-E 1A/2].

sections.heading: nccn_categories
sections.content: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated [VULVA-1]
